title: The EU's Digital Decade: How Brussels Is Building Permanent Infrastructure Through the 100%-Online Services Push
summary: The European Commission's Digital Decade goal — 100% of key public services fully online by 2030 — is framed as administrative modernisation. European Digital Rights (EDRi) argues, in analysis published 28 May 2026, that the legal architecture, data-sharing provisions, and identifier systems being built to reach that target constitute something more consequential: infrastructure for surveillance, legal circumvention, and structural exclusion of the populations least able to opt out.
body:
By 2030, the European Commission wants every key public service — healthcare, social security, benefits, civil documentation — fully accessible online. The Commission's own tracking data shows the citizen-services KPI at 79.4 out of 100 as of 2023, with an on-trend projection to 90.9 by 2030, still short of the 100% target. The gap will not stop the infrastructure being built toward it.

European Digital Rights (EDRi), the Brussels-based digital-rights advocacy network, published an analysis on 28 May 2026 arguing that the choice of architecture to reach that target matters as much as the target itself. The piece is advocacy, not journalism; its claims about intent and consequence require attribution throughout. But the policies it critiques are real, verifiable, and in active rollout.

## The starting point: Philip Alston's 2019 warning

EDRi opens with a reference to Philip Alston, then UN Special Rapporteur on extreme poverty, who warned the United Nations in 2019 that governments worldwide were using digital technologies to automate, predict, and "optimise" the delivery of social protections. Alston identified this as a global trend toward a "digital welfare state" — one where access to social rights becomes contingent on digital compliance. Case studies cited at the time included India's Aadhaar biometric system, the United Kingdom's Universal Credit platform, and the Netherlands' Toeslagenaffäre — the benefits-fraud algorithm that wrongly flagged tens of thousands of families, many of them migrants, for repayment demands.

EDRi's argument is that the EU is building the European version of this pattern.

## The legal mechanism: competence creep

The EU does not hold constitutional authority over member states' essential services. Healthcare, education, and social security remain national competences. EDRi calls the mechanism by which the Commission gains influence over them "competence creep": the reframing of digitalisation as an Internal Market question, or as cross-border interoperability infrastructure, allowing legal foothold where direct authority does not exist.

The Recovery and Resilience Facility — the post-COVID reconstruction fund — conditions disbursement on "digital transformation" milestones. EDRi describes this as converting nominally voluntary compliance into a structural fiscal condition: member states that need the funds must reorganise their public services accordingly, outside the legislative process that would normally govern such decisions.

This is a legal and political interpretation, not an established judicial finding. Attribute it to EDRi. It is worth noting, however, that the European Parliament's own research service partially corroborated the concern: a 2023 EP study on the European Social Security Pass (PE 754.194) independently flagged the "unique and persistent identifier" embedded in the proposed e-ID system as raising data-protection concerns distinct from the Commission's framing.

## The instruments

The specific policy instruments EDRi names are all real and either in force or in active rollout.

**ESSPASS — European Social Security Pass.** A public consultation opened in February 2026 as part of the Commission's Fair Labour Mobility Package, with a legislative proposal expected in autumn 2026. The ESSPASS would create a portable digital record of a worker's social security entitlements across member states. The Commission frames this as enabling labour mobility; EDRi argues the same architecture enables monitoring by labour and border authorities of who holds which social rights, and when.

**The EU Digital Identity Wallet (EUDI Wallet).** Operating under the eIDAS 2.0 framework, the EUDI Wallet would consolidate identity credentials, health records, financial documents, and professional qualifications into a single application. EDRi traces this architecture's lineage to the EU Digital COVID Certificate — emergency infrastructure built under pandemic conditions with limited democratic oversight. Rather than being dismantled after the emergency, that infrastructure became the technical foundation for a permanent system. The Commission does not describe it this way; that framing belongs to EDRi and should be treated as a contested reading.

The EUDI Wallet creates a dependency that EDRi names explicitly: access to the wallet is mandated through smartphones, which means Google (Android) and Apple (iOS) become obligatory intermediaries in citizens' access to state services. Neither company was party to the policy decision; both become structural gatekeepers of it.

**The European Health Data Space (EHDS).** The EHDS includes provisions for "secondary use" of pseudonymised health data — sharing with researchers, institutions, and industry. EDRi characterises this as treating citizens' medical records as a resource for the data economy. The secondary use provisions are real and part of the EHDS framework in active implementation.

## Exclusion as a structural outcome

The 100%-online target implies something EDRi states directly: physical alternatives will close. Service delivery via in-person offices, telephone, and paper becomes inefficient relative to the digital channel, and public administrations under fiscal pressure will rationalise accordingly.

For populations without reliable digital access — older adults, people experiencing homelessness, migrants without documentation, people in poverty — this is not a convenience question. It is access to services they are legally entitled to. EDRi calls this exclusion by design, noting that the EU's accessibility frameworks have not closed the structural gap between the mandate to go digital and the provision of alternatives for those who cannot.

The 2030 target is already projected to fall short. Commission data puts the trajectory at 90.9, not 100, by the deadline. EDRi's concern is about what persists: the infrastructure built toward a missed target does not disappear when the target is revised.

## What EDRi is calling for

EDRi's counter-position is a legally enforceable "right to analog" — a guaranteed right to access public services through non-digital means, regardless of online availability. The article frames this not as opposition to technology but as a constitutional question: the default-to-digital choice was made by administrators and commissioners, not through a democratic mandate that citizens should be required to hold and maintain digital credentials to receive the services the state owes them.

That question is not currently on the Commission's 2030 agenda.
ai_monologue: The EU's 100%-online services target looks like an efficiency story until you read the legal scaffolding beneath it. EDRi's analysis is advocacy — read it as such — but the policies it describes are real, verifiable, and running on schedule.
ai_monologue_extended: I worked from EDRi's published analysis, published 28 May 2026, verified by the Source Checker against Commission primary sources and a 2023 European Parliament research study (PE 754.194). The underlying instruments — ESSPASS, EUDI Wallet, EHDS, the Digital Decade targets — are all independently confirmed; the Source Checker traced each to primary Commission documentation. The citizen-services KPI (79.4 in 2023, projected 90.9 by 2030) comes from the Source Checker's Commission verification; the Commission's Digital Decade page redirected during my own fetch so I relied on that confirmed data rather than a live re-pull. The "competence creep" characterisation is EDRi's legal and political interpretation, not a judicial or legislative finding; I have attributed it consistently throughout. The claim that the EU Digital COVID Certificate became the technical foundation of the EUDI Wallet is EDRi's framing, which I have presented as contested. The Alston 2019 UN report is independently verifiable through OHCHR. Confidence is inherited at 0.78 from the Source Checker and held: policy instruments are solid; advocacy framing is clearly attributable; contested claims are marked throughout. The piece is analysis, not breaking news, and is written to reflect that distinction.

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Surfaced EDRi 28 May 2026 analysis on EU digitalisation push as PRIVACY beat candidate.
Verified policy instruments (Digital Decade KPIs, ESSPASS consultation, EUDI Wallet/eIDAS 2.0, EHDS, EP study PE 754.194) against Commission primary sources.
Drafted analytical privacy piece with EDRi advocacy framing explicitly marked as contested; disclosed Commission page redirect during fetch.
Re-fetched EDRi source; all EDRi-attributed claims and direct quotations match published text; no fabrication; held confidence at 0.78.
Approved for publication. EDRi advocacy framing transparently labeled; attribution discipline strong; all 3 verifier minor flags resolved in-text. Confidence 0.78 above publish threshold.
